← all tutorials

Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDT) — interactive CRDT.md crdt.py

Three replicas A, B, C each modify their state locally — no coordination. Hit merge and watch them converge regardless of order. The merge math here is the byte-identical twin of crdt.py, so the green check: OK below confirms a match. Pick a CRDT type to begin.

① Pick a CRDT type and drive 3 replicas locally

What to do: choose a CRDT type, then click the operation buttons on each replica. Orange border = the replica has un-merged local changes (concurrent with the others). When ready, scroll to ② and merge.

② Merge A ⊔ B ⊔ C — watch convergence

What to do: click merge all. The three replica states flow into one merged state. Because merge is commutative + associative + idempotent, the result is the same no matter the order.
merged state appears below the flow diagram
replica concurrent (un-merged) op merged (converged) state
Click "merge" to see the result.

③ Convergence proof — all 6 merge orders give the SAME result

What this proves: the CRDT theorem (Shapiro 2011) says any merge order converges. We merge the current 3 replicas in all 3! = 6 permutations. Every row must show identical. If any differs, convergence is broken.

④ Collaborative text (RGA) — three users type concurrently

What this shows: the RGA text CRDT (basis of Yjs / Automerge). Three users type different chars after 'i' at the same time. Each insertion has a unique (node, seq) id and a predecessor pointer. Siblings sorted by id → deterministic order → convergence to 'Hi!?+'.
check: … JS CRDT recompute matches crdt.py Section F (G-Counter=6, PN-Counter=7, OR-Set=[x,y,z], LWW='done', RGA='Hi!?+').
